1: Paulos kai Silouanos kai Timotheos tē ekklēsia Thessalonikeon en theō patri hēmōn kai kyriō Iēsou Christō: the same triple authorship as 1 Thessalonians (Paul, Silvanus/Silas, Timothy). The Thessalonian congregation receives its second letter within months or at most a year of the first, apparently because the eschatological confusion addressed in ch.2 has not been resolved by the first letter alone. The unusual double use of en (in God our Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ) parallels 1 Thess 1:1 but is unique in the Pauline corpus.
2: charis hymin kai eirēnē apo theou patros [hēmōn] kai kyriou Iēsou Christou: the standard Pauline grace-and-peace greeting. The expansion hēmōn (our) with Father makes the divine fatherhood a shared possession of apostles and congregation alike — a notable intimacy.
3: Eucharistein opheilomen pantote peri hymōn adelphoi kathōs axion estin hoti hyperauxanei hē pistis hymōn kai pleonazei hē agapē henos hekastou pantōn hymōn eis allēlous: 'We ought always to give thanks for you, brothers, as is right, because your faith is growing abundantly (hyperauxanei) and the love of every one of you for one another is increasing (pleonazei).' Hyperauxanei (growing beyond measure, growing super-abundantly) — the hyper-compound intensifies normal growth to overflow. The letter's thanksgiving period identifies the two developments justifying the thanksgiving: growing faith and multiplying love.
4: hōste autous hēmas en hymin enkauchasthai en tais ekklēsiais tou theou hyper tēs hypomonēs hymōn kai pisteōs en pasin tois diōgmois hymōn kai tais thlipsesin hais anechesthe: 'We ourselves boast about you in the churches of God for your steadfastness (hypomonē) and faith in all your persecutions and in the afflictions that you are enduring.' Hypomonē (steadfast endurance, patient standing-under) is the Christian virtue of holding ground under pressure — not passive resignation but active persistence. The Thessalonian persecutions were real social and possibly legal pressures (1 Thess 2:14-15; Acts 17:5-9).
5: endeigma tēs dikaias kriseōs tou theou eis to kataxiōthēnai hymas tēs basileias tou theou hyper hēs kai paschete: 'This is evidence (endeigma) of the righteous judgment (dikaias kriseōs) of God, that you may be considered worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you are also suffering.' The suffering itself is evidence of the judgment's righteousness — not proof that God has abandoned them but proof of their refinement as genuine members of the coming kingdom. The kingdom for which they suffer is the eschatological reign of God.
6: eiper dikaion para theō antapodounai tois thlibousin hymas thlipsin: 'since indeed it is just (dikaion) with God to repay (antapodounai) with affliction those who afflict you' — the divine lex talionis applied eschatologically. Antapodounai (to give back in return, to repay in full) reflects the OT retributive principle: the measure given will be returned (Isa 66:6; Jer 51:56). God's righteousness demands recompense.
7: kai hymin tois thlibomenois anesin meth' hēmōn en tē apokalypsei tou kyriou Iēsou ap' ouranou met' angelōn dynameōs autou en pyri phlogos: 'and to grant rest (anesin) with us to you who are afflicted, when the Lord Jesus is revealed (apokalypsei) from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire.' Apokalypsis (unveiling, revelation) — the parousia is an apokalypsis (unveiling of the hidden reality) as well as a parousia (presence/coming). En pyri phlogos (in flaming fire) echoes the theophanic imagery of Isa 66:15 (YHWH coming with fire); the divine fire-appearance is now the coming of the Lord Jesus.
8: didontos ekdikēsin tois mē eidosin theon kai tois mē hypakouousin tō euangeliō tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou: 'inflicting vengeance (ekdikēsin) on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus.' Ekdikēsis (vengeance, punishment, judicial satisfaction) — the eschatological judgment as God's executive enforcement of justice. The two groups — those who do not know God (a Gentile category, Jer 10:25; Ps 79:6) and those who do not obey the gospel (a Jewish/religious category) — encompass all who have rejected the divine self-disclosure.
9: hoitines dikēn tisousin olethron aiōnion apo prosōpou tou kyriou kai apo tēs doxēs tēs ischyos autou: 'They will suffer the punishment (dikēn tisousin) of eternal destruction (olethron aiōnion), away from the presence (prosōpou) of the Lord and from the glory of his might.' Olethros aiōnios (eternal/age-long destruction) — not annihilation but permanent separation from the source of life and glory. The phrase apo prosōpou tou kyriou (from the face/presence of the Lord) echoes Isa 2:10, 19, 21 (hide from the terror of the LORD and the splendor of his majesty).
10: hotan elthē endoxasthēnai en tois hagiois autou kai thaumasthēnai en pasin tois pisteusasin: 'when he comes on that day to be glorified in his saints and to be marveled at among all who have believed.' The saints (hagioi) as the location of Christ's glory — his coming is simultaneously the revelation of who they are (v.10: glorified in his saints) and what he is. The marveling (thaumasthēnai) echoes the language of theophanic awe (Isa 66:5 LXX; Ps 68:35).
11: eis ho kai proseuchometha pantote peri hymōn hina hymas axiōsē tēs klēseōs ho theos hēmōn kai plērōsē pasan eudokian agathōsynēs kai ergon pisteōs en dynamei: 'To this end we always pray for you, that our God may make you worthy of his calling (klēseōs) and may fulfill every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power (dynamei).' Eudokia agathōsynēs (resolve for good, good pleasure toward goodness) — a compressed phrase meaning either the Thessalonians' own good resolve or God's good pleasure that produces their goodness. En dynamei (by power) — the eschatological power at work now in the congregation that will be fully manifested at the parousia.
12: hopōs endoxasthē to onoma tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou en hymin kai hymeis en autō kata tēn charin tou theou hēmōn kai kyriou Iēsou Christou: 'so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.' The mutual glorification: the Lord Jesus is glorified in the congregation; the congregation is glorified in him. The Isa 66:5 background continues: the name of the Lord that was mocked will be glorified. The grace of God and the Lord Jesus Christ — the grammatical construction makes God and Jesus Christ a unified source of grace.
